content.assessment: T2's capacity-contraction approach resembles a partial, administrative-lever contraction rather than the full statutory restructuring Poland undertook in either 1999 or 2017. The Polish case demonstrates that structural mechanism matters: Poland's reforms changed the point of tracking itself, whereas T2 only shrinks the size of the pool subject to existing tracking rules, leaving the selection mechanism's distributional logic intact.
opportunities: Being administratively reversible could allow phased or reversible piloting, unlike Poland's structural reforms which were difficult to unwind cleanly once implemented (F-live-polish_reform-04)
strengths: Administrative/discretionary levers (raising thresholds, capacity caps) avoid the need for primary legislation, reducing the kind of abrupt legal reversal risk seen in Poland's 2017 change (F-live-polish_reform-04)
threats: Because it is administratively driven, T2 lacks the durability that only statutory structural change provides; a future administration could reverse capacity constraints as easily as Poland reversed its comprehensive stage (F-live-polish_reform-04)
weaknesses: Contraction without addressing the underlying selection criteria leaves in place the same distributional mechanism that Poland's structural (not just size) reform targeted, per the lens's distributional-effects criterion

No sequencing model is proposed to manage the transition of displaced demand, an omission Poland's abrupt 2017 approach shows can produce acute short-term disruption if unmanaged (F-live-polish_reform-05)
